title: Yemen Mocha: The 500-Year-Old Coffee Tradition ☕📈
description:
Yemen invented coffee culture. The Mocha port, the ancient varietals, the terraced mountain farms, and why Yemeni coffee returned to specialty markets at $80+ per pound.

Sources: SCA Yemen origin reports, James Hoffmann, Sprudge, Mokha Foundation, Port of Mokha.

segments.voice: Coffee was first brewed in Yemen six hundred years ago. The country that invented coffee culture nearly disappeared from the global coffee map. Then a Yemeni-American named Mokhtar Alkhanshali rebuilt the trade during a brutal civil war. His coffee now sells for over eighty dollars per pound at specialty roasters.

The story of coffee starts in Ethiopia, where the plant grew wild. The story of coffee as a drink starts in Yemen. Sufi monks in the fifteenth century brewed coffee to stay awake during late-night prayers. The practice spread from Yemen across the Ottoman Empire, then to Europe, then to the world.

The port of Mocha on the Red Sea coast became the center of global coffee trade. From 1500 to 1700, every coffee bean that reached Europe passed through Mocha. The name became synonymous with coffee. The Mocha-Java blend, still sold today, refers to the historical pairing of these two origins.

Yemen guarded its coffee monopoly fiercely. Yemeni traders would boil or roast all beans before export to prevent cultivation elsewhere. The strategy worked for two hundred years. Then Dutch traders smuggled live plants out in the 1600s. The monopoly collapsed but the Yemeni reputation survived.

Modern Yemen produces tiny quantities of coffee. Total annual production is about ten thousand metric tons, roughly one tenth of one percent of global supply. Brazil produces in two days what Yemen produces in a year. The scarcity drives prices but limits market presence.

Yemeni coffee grows on terraced mountainsides at altitudes from fourteen hundred to twenty-four hundred meters. The terraces have been hand-built across centuries. Farms are tiny, often less than one hectare. Most farms grow coffee alongside qat and food crops. The agriculture is ancient and labor-intensive.

The varietals in Yemen are ancient and unique. Researchers identified several local landraces that exist nowhere else in the world. Names like Udaini, Dawairi, Tuffahi, and Bura'i refer to varietals that predate the Bourbon and Typica selections by hundreds of years. The genetic resource is irreplaceable.

Processing in Yemen uses traditional natural methods. Cherries dry on rooftops, terrace walls, or dedicated drying platforms for two to three weeks. The dry climate of Yemen allows for slow, even drying that develops complex flavors. The minimal water use suits the arid environment.

The cup profile of Yemeni coffee is unique. Expect intense dried fruit, chocolate, spice, leather, tobacco, and wine. The body is heavy. The finish is long. Some lots taste like ancient port wine. The flavor profile resembles no other coffee in the world. Specialty drinkers compare cupping Yemeni to drinking a different beverage entirely.

The Bani Matar district near Sanaa produces some of the most celebrated Yemeni coffee. Farms grow at altitudes around two thousand meters. The terroir produces cups with notes of dates, cocoa, cardamom, and cherry. Bani Matar coffee was famous in European markets in the 1700s.

Haraz, west of Sanaa, is another celebrated region. The Haraz mountains rise dramatically with farms perched on impossible terraces. Cup notes include dried apricot, milk chocolate, almond, and floral spice. Port of Mokha pioneered the modern Haraz specialty market.

Mokhtar Alkhanshali founded Port of Mokha in 2015. The Yemeni-American entrepreneur traveled to Yemen during the civil war to source coffee. His story, documented in Dave Eggers's book The Monk of Mokha, became a publishing phenomenon. Port of Mokha sells some lots for over four hundred dollars per pound.

The Yemeni civil war that started in 2014 devastated coffee production. Saudi-led airstrikes destroyed infrastructure. Naval blockades stopped exports. Many farmers fled or stopped farming. The remaining producers face famine conditions while still tending coffee trees. The trade survives against impossible odds.

Getting Yemeni coffee out of the country requires extraordinary effort. Coffee travels by truck across active war zones to Aden port. From there it ships through controlled corridors. Some shipments take six months from harvest to roaster. Insurance costs are extreme.

Roasters that source Yemeni coffee include Blue Bottle, Counter Culture, Olympia Coffee, and the original Port of Mokha. Expect to pay between forty and eighty dollars per twelve ounce bag for quality Yemeni lots. Some auction lots reach hundreds of dollars per pound.

Brewing Yemeni coffee at home, the heavy body and dried fruit notes shine in French press and pour-over. James Hoffmann recommends ratio one to sixteen for V60, water at ninety-three Celsius, medium-fine grind. Three minute brew time. The cup should taste like a glass of port.

Espresso from Yemeni beans produces intense, syrupy shots with chocolate and dried fruit dominance. The traditional Italian espresso blend would historically include Yemeni Mocha. Modern blends use Brazilian and Indian beans instead, losing the original character of espresso.

Yemeni coffee ceremonies center around qishr, a drink made from dried coffee cherry husks rather than the bean. The husks brew with ginger, cardamom, and cinnamon. The resulting drink tastes nothing like coffee, more like spiced tea. Qishr remains popular across Yemen today.

The varietals of Yemen are genetically distinct from arabica found elsewhere. Studies suggest some Yemeni landraces represent original arabica genetics from before the Yemen-to-Java migration. Preserving these varietals matters for global coffee breeding programs.

The Mokha Foundation, founded by Mokhtar Alkhanshali, supports Yemeni farmers through training, fair pricing, and direct trade. The foundation helps farmers maintain ancient varietals and improve processing quality. The work preserves a coffee tradition older than any other.

Storage and shipping take longer for Yemeni coffee than any other origin. The combination of war, blockades, and remote farm locations means some lots arrive at roasters six to twelve months after harvest. The freshness suffers. Roasters adjust by selecting lots with greater storage stability.

The terraced farming system in Yemen is older than Yemen itself. Some terraces date back two thousand years. The terraces prevent erosion on steep slopes and capture monsoon rainfall. The agricultural infrastructure represents irreplaceable cultural heritage at risk of destruction.

Water scarcity threatens Yemeni coffee long-term. Yemen has one of the lowest per-capita water availability in the world. Coffee competes with qat and food crops for limited water. The pressure pushes some farms to abandon coffee in favor of more profitable qat.

The cup quality of Yemeni coffee varies dramatically. Lots can score from seventy to ninety-two on the SCA scale. The variability reflects the chaotic processing conditions: rooftop drying, inconsistent picking, no standardized infrastructure. Top Yemeni lots are extraordinary but consistency is rare.

But there is something Yemeni coffee buyers rarely emphasize. The premium prices for Yemeni coffee partly reflect war-tax and shipping costs as much as cup quality. The same dried fruit and chocolate profile exists in Sumatran or Ethiopian coffees at one quarter the price. Buyers pay for the story as much as the cup.

To experience authentic Yemeni coffee, look for Bani Matar or Haraz origin lots from named roasters like Port of Mokha. Brew French press or V60. Drink black. The cup will taste ancient. Next video, the James Hoffmann V60 technique that made pour-over a global phenomenon. Subscribe.
